Marketplace Analytics: FoodHub Courier Demand and Sales Optimization¶

Context¶

The number of restaurants in New York is increasing day by day. Lots of students and busy professionals rely on those restaurants due to their hectic lifestyles. Online food delivery service is a great option for them. It provides them with good food from their favorite restaurants. A food aggregator company FoodHub offers access to multiple restaurants through a single smartphone app.

The app allows the restaurants to receive a direct online order from a customer. The app assigns a delivery person from the company to pick up the order after it is confirmed by the restaurant. The delivery person then uses the map to reach the restaurant and waits for the food package. Once the food package is handed over to the delivery person, he/she confirms the pick-up in the app and travels to the customer's location to deliver the food. The delivery person confirms the drop-off in the app after delivering the food package to the customer. The customer can rate the order in the app. The food aggregator earns money by collecting a fixed margin of the delivery order from the restaurants.

Objective¶

The food aggregator company has stored the data of the different orders made by the registered customers in their online portal. They want to analyze the data to get a fair idea about the demand of different restaurants which will help them in enhancing their customer experience. Suppose you are hired as a Data Scientist in this company and the Data Science team has shared some of the key questions that need to be answered. Perform the data analysis to find answers to these questions that will help the company to improve the business.

Data Description¶

The data contains the different data related to a food order. The detailed data dictionary is given below.

Data Dictionary¶

  • order_id: Unique ID of the order
  • customer_id: ID of the customer who ordered the food
  • restaurant_name: Name of the restaurant
  • cuisine_type: Cuisine ordered by the customer
  • cost: Cost of the order
  • day_of_the_week: Indicates whether the order is placed on a weekday or weekend (The weekday is from Monday to Friday and the weekend is Saturday and Sunday)
  • rating: Rating given by the customer out of 5
  • food_preparation_time: Time (in minutes) taken by the restaurant to prepare the food. This is calculated by taking the difference between the timestamps of the restaurant's order confirmation and the delivery person's pick-up confirmation.
  • delivery_time: Time (in minutes) taken by the delivery person to deliver the food package. This is calculated by taking the difference between the timestamps of the delivery person's pick-up confirmation and drop-off information
In [2]:
# importing libraries for data manipulation
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import sqlite3

# importing libraries for data visualization
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
In [3]:
# read the data
from google.colab import drive
drive.mount('/content/drive')

Mounted at /content/drive
In [4]:
df = pd.read_csv('/content/drive/MyDrive/DSAAI Notebooks and Learning Material/foodhub_order.csv')
# returns the first 5 rows
df.head()
Out[4]:
order_id customer_id restaurant_name cuisine_type cost_of_the_order day_of_the_week rating food_preparation_time delivery_time
0 1477147 337525 Hangawi Korean 30.75 Weekend Not given 25 20
1 1477685 358141 Blue Ribbon Sushi Izakaya Japanese 12.08 Weekend Not given 25 23
2 1477070 66393 Cafe Habana Mexican 12.23 Weekday 5 23 28
3 1477334 106968 Blue Ribbon Fried Chicken American 29.20 Weekend 3 25 15
4 1478249 76942 Dirty Bird to Go American 11.59 Weekday 4 25 24
In [5]:
# Creating a SQLite database file
conn = sqlite3.connect('foodhub_order.db')
In [6]:
# Write the dataframe into SQLite as a table
df.to_sql("orders", conn, if_exists="replace", index=False)

print("Rows loaded into SQLite:", len(df))

Rows loaded into SQLite: 1898

After loading the raw dataset into SQLite, the next EDA step inspects structural properties of the table through SQL. Schema inspection, categorical frequency counts, and numerical summaries are performed at the database layer before deeper analysis continues in Python.

In [7]:
# Checking data info
query = """
SELECT *
FROM orders
LIMIT 10;
"""

pd.read_sql(query, conn)

In [ ]:
# Describing data
schema_query = """
PRAGMA table_info(orders);
"""

pd.read_sql(schema_query, conn)
Out[ ]:
cid name type notnull dflt_value pk
0 0 order_id INTEGER 0 None 0
1 1 customer_id INTEGER 0 None 0
2 2 restaurant_name TEXT 0 None 0
3 3 cuisine_type TEXT 0 None 0
4 4 cost_of_the_order REAL 0 None 0
5 5 day_of_the_week TEXT 0 None 0
6 6 rating TEXT 0 None 0
7 7 food_preparation_time INTEGER 0 None 0
8 8 delivery_time INTEGER 0 None 0
In [ ]:
# Checking row count, distinct customers, distinct restaurants
eda_overview_query = """
SELECT
    COUNT(*) AS total_orders,
    COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) AS unique_customers,
    COUNT(DISTINCT restaurant_name) AS unique_restaurants,
    COUNT(DISTINCT cuisine_type) AS unique_cuisines
FROM orders;
"""

pd.read_sql(eda_overview_query, conn)
Out[ ]:
total_orders unique_customers unique_restaurants unique_cuisines
0 1898 1200 178 14
In [ ]:
# Checking how many unique customers placed orders
unique_customers_query = """
SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) AS unique_customers
FROM orders;
"""

pd.read_sql(unique_customers_query, conn)
Out[ ]:
unique_customers
0 1200
In [ ]:
# Checking min, max and average values for cost, prep and delivery times
numeric_summary_query = """
SELECT
    COUNT(*) AS total_rows,

    MIN(cost_of_the_order) AS min_cost,
    ROUND(AVG(cost_of_the_order), 2) AS avg_cost,
    MAX(cost_of_the_order) AS max_cost,

    MIN(food_preparation_time) AS min_prep_time,
    ROUND(AVG(food_preparation_time), 2) AS avg_prep_time,
    MAX(food_preparation_time) AS max_prep_time,

    MIN(delivery_time) AS min_delivery_time,
    ROUND(AVG(delivery_time), 2) AS avg_delivery_time,
    MAX(delivery_time) AS max_delivery_time
FROM orders;
"""

pd.read_sql(numeric_summary_query, conn)
Out[ ]:
total_rows min_cost avg_cost max_cost min_prep_time avg_prep_time max_prep_time min_delivery_time avg_delivery_time max_delivery_time
0 1898 4.47 16.5 35.41 20 27.37 35 15 24.16 33

Observations:¶

The dataset is void of any missing values. The 'ratings' column does contain strings 'Not given' which will be treated accordingly throughout EDA to support insights (some problems will take into account the measure of 'Not given' and some will exclude rows containing them).

Four of the columns are categorical data: 'restaurant_name', 'cuisine type', 'day_of_the_week' and 'rating'.

The remaining five are currently numerical data: 'order_id', 'customer_id', 'cost_of_the_order', 'food_preparation_time' and 'delivery_time'.

There are more orders recorded than there are unique customers, indicating that there is are repeat customers within the data.

Quick check on data yields these windows:

Cost ranges from 4.47 to 35.41 dollars with an average of 16.5 dollars

Prep time ranges from 20 to 35 minutes with an average of 27.37 minutes

Delivery time ranges from 15 to 33 minutes with an average of 24.16 minutes

In [ ]:
# Checking order count by cuisine type
cuisine_dist_query = """
SELECT
    cuisine_type,
    COUNT(*) AS order_count
FROM orders
GROUP BY cuisine_type
ORDER BY order_count DESC;
"""

pd.read_sql(cuisine_dist_query, conn)
Out[ ]:
cuisine_type order_count
0 American 584
1 Japanese 470
2 Italian 298
3 Chinese 215
4 Mexican 77
5 Indian 73
6 Middle Eastern 49
7 Mediterranean 46
8 Thai 19
9 French 18
10 Southern 17
11 Korean 13
12 Spanish 12
13 Vietnamese 7
In [ ]:
# Visualizing order counts by cuisine type
sns.histplot(df['cuisine_type'])
plt.xticks(rotation=90)
plt.title("Cuisine Type Count")
plt.show()
In [ ]:
# Checking distribution of orders on weekday vs weekend
day_mix_query = """
SELECT
    day_of_the_week,
    COUNT(*) AS order_count,
    ROUND(100.0 * COUNT(*) / (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders), 2) AS pct_of_total_orders
FROM orders
GROUP BY day_of_the_week
ORDER BY order_count DESC;
"""

pd.read_sql(day_mix_query, conn)
Out[ ]:
day_of_the_week order_count pct_of_total_orders
0 Weekend 1351 71.18
1 Weekday 547 28.82
In [ ]:
# Showing counts on orders placed on weekdays vs orders placed on weekends
sns.histplot(df['day_of_the_week'])
plt.title("Weekend vs Weekday")
plt.show()
In [ ]:
# Checking which cuisines are most popular on weekends
weekend_cuisine_query = """
SELECT
    cuisine_type,
    COUNT(*) AS weekend_orders
FROM orders
WHERE day_of_the_week = 'Weekend'
GROUP BY cuisine_type
ORDER BY weekend_orders DESC;
"""

pd.read_sql(weekend_cuisine_query, conn)
Out[ ]:
cuisine_type weekend_orders
0 American 415
1 Japanese 335
2 Italian 207
3 Chinese 163
4 Mexican 53
5 Indian 49
6 Middle Eastern 32
7 Mediterranean 32
8 Thai 15
9 French 13
10 Spanish 11
11 Southern 11
12 Korean 11
13 Vietnamese 4
In [ ]:
# Checking which cuisines are most popular on weekdays
weekday_cuisine_query = """
SELECT
    cuisine_type,
    COUNT(*) AS weekday_orders
FROM orders
WHERE day_of_the_week = 'Weekday'
GROUP BY cuisine_type
ORDER BY weekday_orders DESC;
"""

pd.read_sql(weekday_cuisine_query, conn)
Out[ ]:
cuisine_type weekday_orders
0 American 169
1 Japanese 135
2 Italian 91
3 Chinese 52
4 Mexican 24
5 Indian 24
6 Middle Eastern 17
7 Mediterranean 14
8 Southern 6
9 French 5
10 Thai 4
11 Vietnamese 3
12 Korean 2
13 Spanish 1
In [ ]:
# Comparing cuisine demand across weekday vs weekend in one table
cuisine_day_compare_query = """
SELECT
    cuisine_type,
    SUM(CASE WHEN day_of_the_week = 'Weekday' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS weekday_orders,
    SUM(CASE WHEN day_of_the_week = 'Weekend' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS weekend_orders,
    COUNT(*) AS total_orders
FROM orders
GROUP BY cuisine_type
ORDER BY total_orders DESC;
"""

pd.read_sql(cuisine_day_compare_query, conn)
Out[ ]:
cuisine_type weekday_orders weekend_orders total_orders
0 American 169 415 584
1 Japanese 135 335 470
2 Italian 91 207 298
3 Chinese 52 163 215
4 Mexican 24 53 77
5 Indian 24 49 73
6 Middle Eastern 17 32 49
7 Mediterranean 14 32 46
8 Thai 4 15 19
9 French 5 13 18
10 Southern 6 11 17
11 Korean 2 11 13
12 Spanish 1 11 12
13 Vietnamese 3 4 7
In [ ]:
# Visualizing order counts by cuisine on weekends
sns.countplot(data=df[df['day_of_the_week'] == 'Weekend'], x='cuisine_type')
plt.xticks(rotation=90)
plt.title("Cuisine Type Count (Weekend Only)")
plt.show()
In [ ]:
# Checking order counts on each cuisine again
sns.histplot(df['cuisine_type'])
plt.xticks(rotation=90)
plt.title("Cuisine Type Count")
plt.show()
In [8]:
# Visualizing notable differences between weekdays and weekends
sns.countplot(data=df, x = 'cuisine_type', hue = 'day_of_the_week')
plt.xticks(rotation=90)
plt.title("Cuisine Type Count")
plt.show()

Observations:¶

Weekend orders make up a disproportionately large share of total volume despite covering fewer days, indicating a strong demand concentration during this period.

Cuisine preferences remain largely stable across the week, with American, Japanese, Italian, and Chinese consistently leading in order volume.

While lower-ranked cuisines exhibit minor shifts in ordering position between weekdays and weekends, these changes are marginal and do not materially affect overall demand patterns.

Weekend demand not only increases in total volume but is also more heavily concentrated within the top cuisines, with American cuisine in particular showing the strongest dominance.

In [ ]:
# Checking which individual restaurants receive the most orders
top_restaurants_query = """
SELECT
    restaurant_name,
    COUNT(*) AS order_count
FROM orders
GROUP BY restaurant_name
ORDER BY order_count DESC
LIMIT 10;
"""

pd.read_sql(top_restaurants_query, conn)
Out[ ]:
restaurant_name order_count
0 Shake Shack 219
1 The Meatball Shop 132
2 Blue Ribbon Sushi 119
3 Blue Ribbon Fried Chicken 96
4 Parm 68
5 RedFarm Broadway 59
6 RedFarm Hudson 55
7 TAO 49
8 Han Dynasty 46
9 Blue Ribbon Sushi Bar & Grill 44
In [ ]:
# Creating a visual of order counts from top 5 restaurants in dataset
top5 = df['restaurant_name'].value_counts().head(5)
top5_df = top5.reset_index()
top5_df.columns = ['restaurant_name', 'count']
sns.barplot(data=top5_df, x='restaurant_name', y='count')

plt.xticks(rotation=45, ha='right')
plt.title("Top 5 Most Frequent Restaurants")
plt.xlabel("Restaurant Name")
plt.ylabel("Count")

plt.show()

Key Takeaways:

Customers

The number of unique customers is lower than the total number of orders, indicating repeat ordering behavior within the customer base.

Day mix

Order volume is substantially concentrated on weekends, suggesting stronger peak demand and untapped weekday potential.

Cuisines

Cuisine demand remains concentrated in a small set of categories, with American appearing especially prominent in the weekend mix.

Popular restaurants

The top five restaurants are Shake Shack, The Meatball Shop, Blue Ribbon Sushi, Blue Ribbon Fried Chicken and Parm, with rankings respective to that order. Each of these restaurants align with the most popular cuisines.

Time Analysis¶

In [ ]:
timing_summary_query = """
SELECT
    ROUND(AVG(food_preparation_time), 2) AS avg_prep_time,
    MIN(food_preparation_time) AS min_prep_time,
    MAX(food_preparation_time) AS max_prep_time,

    ROUND(AVG(delivery_time), 2) AS avg_delivery_time,
    MIN(delivery_time) AS min_delivery_time,
    MAX(delivery_time) AS max_delivery_time,

    ROUND(AVG(food_preparation_time + delivery_time), 2) AS avg_total_fulfillment_time,
    MIN(food_preparation_time + delivery_time) AS min_total_fulfillment_time,
    MAX(food_preparation_time + delivery_time) AS max_total_fulfillment_time
FROM orders;
"""

pd.read_sql(timing_summary_query, conn)
Out[ ]:
avg_prep_time min_prep_time max_prep_time avg_delivery_time min_delivery_time max_delivery_time avg_total_fulfillment_time min_total_fulfillment_time max_total_fulfillment_time
0 27.37 20 35 24.16 15 33 51.53 35 68

Observations:¶

Minimum amount of preparation time - 20 minutes

Average amount of preparation time - ~27.372 minutes

Maximum amount of preparation time - 35 minutes.

In [ ]:
# Visualizing the distribution of food preparation times
sns.histplot(df['food_preparation_time'], bins=16)
plt.title("Food Preparation Time")
plt.xticks(range(20, 36, 1))
plt.show()
In [ ]:
sns.kdeplot(df['food_preparation_time'])
plt.title("Food Preparation Time")
Out[ ]:
Text(0.5, 1.0, 'Food Preparation Time')
In [ ]:
sns.boxplot(data = df, x ='food_preparation_time')
plt.title("Food Preparation Time")
plt.show()
In [ ]:
sns.histplot(df['delivery_time'])
plt.title("Delivery Time")
Out[ ]:
Text(0.5, 1.0, 'Delivery Time')
In [ ]:
sns.kdeplot(df['delivery_time'])
plt.title("Delivery Time")
Out[ ]:
Text(0.5, 1.0, 'Delivery Time')
In [ ]:
sns.boxplot(data = df, x ='delivery_time')
plt.title("Delivery Time")
Out[ ]:
Text(0.5, 1.0, 'Delivery Time')

Observations:¶

Food preparation time ranges from 20 to 35 minutes, with a fairly consistent distribution throughout and a slight peak in the 21-22 minute range.

Data on delivery times shows one peak of times around 18-21 minutes, and a following larger peak around 25-30 minutes, indicating two typical delivery speeds.

Data is somewhat tightly packed, with over 50% of times falling between 20 and 28 mintues.

In [ ]:
timing_by_day_query = """
SELECT
    day_of_the_week,
    COUNT(*) AS order_count,
    ROUND(AVG(food_preparation_time), 2) AS avg_prep_time,
    ROUND(AVG(delivery_time), 2) AS avg_delivery_time,
    ROUND(AVG(food_preparation_time + delivery_time), 2) AS avg_total_fulfillment_time
FROM orders
GROUP BY day_of_the_week
ORDER BY order_count DESC;
"""

pd.read_sql(timing_by_day_query, conn)
Out[ ]:
day_of_the_week order_count avg_prep_time avg_delivery_time avg_total_fulfillment_time
0 Weekend 1351 27.44 22.47 49.91
1 Weekday 547 27.21 28.34 55.55
In [ ]:
# Checking which cuisines are the most consistent with timing
timing_by_cuisine_query = """
SELECT
    cuisine_type,
    COUNT(*) AS order_count,
    ROUND(AVG(food_preparation_time), 2) AS avg_prep_time,
    ROUND(AVG(delivery_time), 2) AS avg_delivery_time,
    ROUND(AVG(food_preparation_time + delivery_time), 2) AS avg_total_fulfillment_time
FROM orders
GROUP BY cuisine_type
HAVING COUNT(*) >= 10
ORDER BY avg_total_fulfillment_time ASC;
"""

pd.read_sql(timing_by_cuisine_query, conn)
Out[ ]:
cuisine_type order_count avg_prep_time avg_delivery_time avg_total_fulfillment_time
0 Korean 13 25.46 20.92 46.38
1 Spanish 12 26.92 23.42 50.33
2 Thai 19 27.32 23.16 50.47
3 Mediterranean 46 27.00 23.59 50.59
4 Middle Eastern 49 26.67 24.08 50.76
5 Mexican 77 26.73 24.39 51.12
6 Indian 73 27.11 24.08 51.19
7 Chinese 215 27.51 23.86 51.37
8 Southern 17 27.59 23.82 51.41
9 American 584 27.44 24.19 51.63
10 Japanese 470 27.51 24.13 51.64
11 Italian 298 27.48 24.57 52.05
12 French 18 26.89 25.33 52.22
In [ ]:
# Checking which restaurants are the most consistent with timing
restaurant_timing_query = """
SELECT
    restaurant_name,
    COUNT(*) AS order_count,
    ROUND(AVG(food_preparation_time), 2) AS avg_prep_time,
    ROUND(AVG(delivery_time), 2) AS avg_delivery_time,
    ROUND(AVG(food_preparation_time + delivery_time), 2) AS avg_total_time
FROM orders
GROUP BY restaurant_name
HAVING COUNT(*) >= 5
ORDER BY avg_total_time ASC
LIMIT 15;
"""

pd.read_sql(restaurant_timing_query, conn)
Out[ ]:
restaurant_name order_count avg_prep_time avg_delivery_time avg_total_time
0 Cho Dang Gol 6 23.83 19.33 43.17
1 Delicatessen 5 27.20 19.40 46.60
2 Westville Hudson 15 25.67 21.73 47.40
3 Benihana 5 23.80 24.40 48.20
4 Cafe Mogador 18 25.28 22.94 48.22
5 Five Guys Burgers and Fries 29 25.62 23.41 49.03
6 Waverly Diner 7 25.00 24.14 49.14
7 Blue Ribbon Sushi Bar & Grill 44 26.30 23.41 49.70
8 Sarabeth's Restaurant 7 23.71 26.00 49.71
9 Vanessa's Dumpling House 9 25.56 24.22 49.78
10 Chipotle Mexican Grill $1.99 Delivery 23 26.00 23.87 49.87
11 TAO 49 26.78 23.16 49.94
12 Hill Country Fried Chicken 14 27.36 22.64 50.00
13 The Smile 18 26.11 23.89 50.00
14 Sushi of Gari 16 28.19 21.94 50.13
In [ ]:
# Checking food preparation time by day of the week
sns.boxplot(data=df, x='day_of_the_week', y='food_preparation_time')
plt.title("Food Preparation Time by Day of the Week")
Out[ ]:
Text(0.5, 1.0, 'Food Preparation Time by Day of the Week')
In [ ]:
# Checking food delivery time by day of the week
sns.boxplot(data=df, x='day_of_the_week', y='delivery_time')
plt.title("Delivery Time Weekends vs Weekdays")
Out[ ]:
Text(0.5, 1.0, 'Delivery Time Weekends vs Weekdays')
In [ ]:
# Creating a new column representative of total time for customer to receive order
df['total_time'] = df['food_preparation_time'] + df['delivery_time']
# Checking total waiting time by day of the week
sns.boxplot(data=df, x='day_of_the_week', y='total_time')
Out[ ]:
<Axes: xlabel='day_of_the_week', ylabel='total_time'>
In [ ]:
# Checking the mean value of entries for delivery time
df['delivery_time'].mean()
Out[ ]:
np.float64(24.161749209694417)

Observations:¶

The average delivery time for all orders is 24.16 minutes.

Comparing fulfillment metrics across weekday and weekend periods helps test whether weaker weekday demand may be associated with slower service times.

Weekend food preparation time strongly mirrors weekdays, suggesting businesses are staffing and preparing accordingly to keep up with higher weekend demand.

In [ ]:
timing_by_day_query = """
SELECT
    day_of_the_week,
    COUNT(*) AS order_count,
    ROUND(AVG(food_preparation_time), 2) AS avg_prep_time,
    ROUND(AVG(delivery_time), 2) AS avg_delivery_time,
    ROUND(AVG(food_preparation_time + delivery_time), 2) AS avg_total_fulfillment_time
FROM orders
GROUP BY day_of_the_week
ORDER BY order_count DESC;
"""

pd.read_sql(timing_by_day_query, conn)
Out[ ]:
day_of_the_week order_count avg_prep_time avg_delivery_time avg_total_fulfillment_time
0 Weekend 1351 27.44 22.47 49.91
1 Weekday 547 27.21 28.34 55.55
In [ ]:
sns.boxplot (data=df, x='cuisine_type', y='food_preparation_time')
plt.title("Cuisine by Preparation Time")
plt.xticks(rotation=90);

Observations:¶

Korean has the most consistently fast preparation times, with a small range and low median.

Thai, Southern, and Spanish and Vietnamese hold the widest range of prep times.

Thai, Southern, Mediterranean and French have dishes that occasionaly take 35 minutes or more.

Most cuisines cluster around a similar median prep time of 26-29 minutes.

Weekends show an average and range of notably shorter delivery time than weekdays. With food preparation times remaining the same, one can reason that delivery drivers are moving faster to keep up with higher demand on weekends.

Cost¶

In [ ]:
# Checking percentage of orders over $20
high_value_order_query = """
SELECT
    COUNT(*) AS total_orders,
    SUM(CASE WHEN cost_of_the_order > 20 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS orders_above_20,
    ROUND(
        100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN cost_of_the_order > 20 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) / COUNT(*),
        2
    ) AS pct_orders_above_20
FROM orders;
"""

pd.read_sql(high_value_order_query, conn)
Out[ ]:
total_orders orders_above_20 pct_orders_above_20
0 1898 555 29.24
In [ ]:
# Checking which cuisines generate the most revenue
cuisine_revenue_query = """
SELECT
    cuisine_type,
    COUNT(*) AS order_count,
    ROUND(SUM(cost_of_the_order), 2) AS total_revenue,
    ROUND(AVG(cost_of_the_order), 2) AS avg_order_value
FROM orders
GROUP BY cuisine_type
ORDER BY total_revenue DESC;
"""

pd.read_sql(cuisine_revenue_query, conn)
Out[ ]:
cuisine_type order_count total_revenue avg_order_value
0 American 584 9530.78 16.32
1 Japanese 470 7663.13 16.30
2 Italian 298 4892.77 16.42
3 Chinese 215 3505.62 16.31
4 Mexican 77 1303.85 16.93
5 Indian 73 1235.14 16.92
6 Middle Eastern 49 922.21 18.82
7 Mediterranean 46 711.84 15.47
8 Thai 19 364.95 19.21
9 French 18 356.29 19.79
10 Southern 17 328.11 19.30
11 Spanish 12 227.93 18.99
12 Korean 13 182.02 14.00
13 Vietnamese 7 90.18 12.88
In [ ]:
sns.histplot(df['cost_of_the_order'])
plt.title("Cost")
Out[ ]:
Text(0.5, 1.0, 'Cost')
In [ ]:
sns.kdeplot(df['cost_of_the_order'])
plt.title("Cost")
Out[ ]:
Text(0.5, 1.0, 'Cost')
In [ ]:
sns.boxplot(data = df, x ='cost_of_the_order')
plt.title("Cost")
Out[ ]:
Text(0.5, 1.0, 'Cost')
In [ ]:
# Checking the percentage of times the condition is true (>$20).
(df['cost_of_the_order'] > 20).mean() * 100
Out[ ]:
np.float64(29.24130663856691)

Observations:¶

Data on order costs shows a positive skew with a long right tail extending towards the 30-40 dollar range.

Most orders are moderately priced with 50% falling in the 12-20 dollar range.

Data is multi-modal with clusterings around 10-12 dollars, 15-18 dollars and 25-30 dollars. The dominant peak lays within the 13-14 dollar range.

Just over 29% of orders are above $20

In [ ]:
# Visualizing cost breakdowns by cuisine and day of week
sns.barplot (data=df, x='cuisine_type', y='cost_of_the_order', hue = 'day_of_the_week')
plt.title("Cost : Cuisine : Weekend vs Weekday")
plt.xticks(rotation=90);

Thai, Southern and French show the highest average costs.¶

Vietnamese has the lowest average costs around 13 to 14 dollars.¶

Most cuisines fall in the 16 to 18 dollar range.¶

Several cuisines; most notably Thai, Spanish and Korean show a higher ratio of average weekend costs than other cuisines (more weekend than weekday), but it is not a strong difference.¶

Cost x Time¶

In [ ]:
# Creating a heatmap to visualize correlation strengths between cost, delivery time and food preparation time.
numeric_df = df[["cost_of_the_order", "food_preparation_time", "delivery_time"]]

corr = numeric_df.corr()

plt.figure(figsize=(12,8))
sns.heatmap(corr, annot=True, cmap='coolwarm')
plt.title("Original data / Cost - Time Correlation")
plt.show()

Observations:¶

Of the numerical data available, there are no immediately visible strong correlations. Time does not seem to have a large affect on cost.¶

Within the entries containing ratings, there also seems to lack any strong correlation between rating, time and cost.¶

Longer wait times technically do produce generally lower ratings, but the findings are slight.¶

Higher costs technically produce generally higher ratings, but the findings aren't noteworthy.¶

In [ ]:
# Checking orders with ratings vs orders without
rating_quality_query = """
SELECT
    COUNT(*) AS total_orders,
    SUM(CASE WHEN rating = 'Not given' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS ratings_not_given,
    SUM(CASE WHEN rating != 'Not given' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS ratings_recorded
FROM orders;
"""

pd.read_sql(rating_quality_query, conn)
Out[ ]:
total_orders ratings_not_given ratings_recorded
0 1898 736 1162

Key Takeaways:¶

Food preparation time¶

There is a consistent spread in food preparation time ranging from 20-36 minutes

Delivery time¶

There is a set of peaks at 5 minute intervals (15, 20, 25) ending with a highest peak at around 28 minutes

Cost¶

We can see a peak in the 13-14 dollar range, followed by a second upswing around 30 dollars (perhaps orders for two persons).

Ratings¶

Nearly 39% of orders are without ratings.

Cuisine¶

American, Japanese, Italian and Chinese are the four most popular cuisines, in that respective order. The top 5 most-ordered-from restaurants fall within those categories.

Additional notes¶

If Blue Ribbon Sushi and Blue Ribbon Fried Chicken belong to the same parent company; Blue Ribbons full sales are near equivalent to Shake Shack, indicating a cornering of the market. Our top 4 cuisines hold near 80% of all orders.

Ratings¶

In [ ]:
# Checking the counts of ratings
sns.countplot(df['rating'])
plt.title("Rating Count")
plt.show()
In [ ]:
# Visualizing order ratings by cost
sns.boxplot (data=df, x='rating', y='cost_of_the_order')
plt.title("Ratings by Cost")
Out[ ]:
Text(0.5, 1.0, 'Ratings by Cost')
In [ ]:
# Visualizing order ratings by delivery time
sns.boxplot (data=df, x='rating', y='delivery_time')
plt.title("Ratings by Delivery Time")
Out[ ]:
Text(0.5, 1.0, 'Ratings by Delivery Time')
In [ ]:
# Visualizing order ratings by food prep time
sns.boxplot (data=df, x='rating', y='food_preparation_time')
plt.title("Ratings by Preparation Time")
Out[ ]:
Text(0.5, 1.0, 'Ratings by Preparation Time')
In [ ]:
# Visualizing ratings by total waiting time
sns.boxplot (data=df, x='rating', y='total_time')
plt.title("Ratings by Total Time")
Out[ ]:
Text(0.5, 1.0, 'Ratings by Total Time')

Observations:¶

Cost and all breakdowns of waiting time generally don't show strong correlations with rating. We see just as tightly packed a middle 50% of observations labeled 'Not Given' in ratings as we do 4 and 5 stars.¶

In [ ]:
# Rechecking ratings coverage
ratings_coverage_query = """
SELECT
    COUNT(*) AS total_orders,
    SUM(CASE WHEN rating = 'Not given' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS ratings_missing,
    SUM(CASE WHEN rating != 'Not given' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS ratings_recorded,
    ROUND(
        100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN rating != 'Not given' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) / COUNT(*),
        2
    ) AS pct_orders_with_rating
FROM orders;
"""

pd.read_sql(ratings_coverage_query, conn)
Out[ ]:
total_orders ratings_missing ratings_recorded pct_orders_with_rating
0 1898 736 1162 61.22
In [ ]:
rating_by_cuisine_query = """
SELECT
    cuisine_type,
    COUNT(*) AS rated_orders,
    ROUND(AVG(CAST(rating AS FLOAT)), 2) AS avg_rating
FROM orders
WHERE rating != 'Not given'
GROUP BY cuisine_type
HAVING COUNT(*) >= 10
ORDER BY avg_rating DESC, rated_orders DESC;
"""

pd.read_sql(rating_by_cuisine_query, conn)
Out[ ]:
cuisine_type rated_orders avg_rating
0 Indian 50 4.54
1 Mexican 48 4.42
2 Japanese 273 4.37
3 Italian 172 4.36
4 Chinese 133 4.34
5 Southern 13 4.31
6 American 368 4.30
7 French 10 4.30
8 Middle Eastern 34 4.24
9 Mediterranean 32 4.22
In [ ]:
# Checking rating distributions by cuisine
sns.countplot(data = df , x = 'cuisine_type', hue = 'rating')
plt.xticks(rotation=90)
plt.title("Ratings Distributions by Cuisine")
plt.show()
In [ ]:
# Average rating among recorded ratings
avg_rating_query = """
SELECT
    COUNT(*) AS rated_orders,
    ROUND(AVG(CAST(rating AS FLOAT)), 2) AS avg_rating,
    MIN(CAST(rating AS FLOAT)) AS min_rating,
    MAX(CAST(rating AS FLOAT)) AS max_rating
FROM orders
WHERE rating != 'Not given';
"""

pd.read_sql(avg_rating_query, conn)
Out[ ]:
rated_orders avg_rating min_rating max_rating
0 1162 4.34 3.0 5.0
In [ ]:
# Seeing if faster restaurants tend to rate better
rating_by_speed_band_query = """
SELECT
    CASE
        WHEN (food_preparation_time + delivery_time) < 40 THEN 'Under 40 min'
        WHEN (food_preparation_time + delivery_time) BETWEEN 40 AND 49 THEN '40-49 min'
        WHEN (food_preparation_time + delivery_time) BETWEEN 50 AND 59 THEN '50-59 min'
        ELSE '60+ min'
    END AS fulfillment_band,
    COUNT(*) AS rated_orders,
    ROUND(AVG(CAST(rating AS FLOAT)), 2) AS avg_rating
FROM orders
WHERE rating != 'Not given'
GROUP BY fulfillment_band
ORDER BY
    CASE fulfillment_band
        WHEN 'Under 40 min' THEN 1
        WHEN '40-49 min' THEN 2
        WHEN '50-59 min' THEN 3
        ELSE 4
    END;
"""

pd.read_sql(rating_by_speed_band_query, conn)
Out[ ]:
fulfillment_band rated_orders avg_rating
0 Under 40 min 47 4.38
1 40-49 min 398 4.36
2 50-59 min 571 4.32
3 60+ min 146 4.37
In [ ]:
# Counting number of unique restaurants per cuisine
restaurants_per_cuisine = df.groupby("cuisine_type")["restaurant_name"].nunique()

# Building rating counts per cuisine
rating_counts = df.pivot_table(
    index="cuisine_type",
    columns="rating",
    aggfunc="size",
    fill_value=0
)

# Applying weights by dividing each row by number of restaurants in that cuisine
weighted_rating_counts = rating_counts.div(restaurants_per_cuisine, axis=0)

weighted_rating_counts

# Generating a visual of correlation matrix
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 8))
sns.heatmap(
    weighted_rating_counts,
    annot=True,
    cmap="YlGnBu",
    fmt=".2f"
)
plt.title("Weighted Ratings per Restaurant by Cuisine")
plt.ylabel("Cuisine")
plt.xlabel("Rating Score")
plt.show()

Observations:¶

The chart above shows the average amount of 3, 4, 5 and empty ratings received per restaurant by cuisine type. For example, your average American restaurant receives 8.97 ratings (summing 1.56, 3.17 and 4.24), whereas your average Korean restaurant receives 1.8 ratings (summing 0.4, 0.8 and 0.6).¶
We have already proven American, Japanese, and Chinese cuisines are 3 of the 4 leading in popular demand. The chart above shows they also each receive considerably higher amounts of reviews per restaurant than other cuisines.¶
A higher volume of restaurants produces a higher volume of orders and thus a higher opportunity for ratings.¶
In [ ]:
# Creating a table that groups amounts of 3-star, 4-star, 5-star and N/A by restaurant.
rating_summary = (
    df.groupby("restaurant_name")["rating"]
      .value_counts()
      .unstack(fill_value=0)
      .reset_index()
)
rating_summary
Out[ ]:
rating restaurant_name 3 4 5 Not given
0 'wichcraft 0 0 1 0
1 12 Chairs 0 1 1 2
2 5 Napkin Burger 1 0 1 3
3 67 Burger 0 0 1 0
4 Alidoro 0 0 0 1
... ... ... ... ... ...
173 Zero Otto Nove 0 1 0 1
174 brgr 1 0 0 1
175 da Umberto 0 0 1 2
176 ilili Restaurant 2 7 4 5
177 indikitch 0 1 1 2

178 rows × 5 columns

In [ ]:
# Summing rating groups for total ratings given
rating_summary["total_ratings"] = rating_summary[["3", "4", "5"]].sum(axis=1)
rating_summary
Out[ ]:
rating restaurant_name 3 4 5 Not given total_ratings
0 'wichcraft 0 0 1 0 1
1 12 Chairs 0 1 1 2 2
2 5 Napkin Burger 1 0 1 3 2
3 67 Burger 0 0 1 0 1
4 Alidoro 0 0 0 1 0
... ... ... ... ... ... ...
173 Zero Otto Nove 0 1 0 1 1
174 brgr 1 0 0 1 1
175 da Umberto 0 0 1 2 1
176 ilili Restaurant 2 7 4 5 13
177 indikitch 0 1 1 2 2

178 rows × 6 columns

In [ ]:
# Creating series with total order counts by restaurant
order_counts = df["restaurant_name"].value_counts()

# Adding total order counts as a new column in rating_summary
rating_summary = rating_summary.set_index("restaurant_name")
rating_summary["order_count"] = order_counts
rating_summary = rating_summary.reset_index()
In [ ]:
# Finding average *amount* of ratings per order by dividing total ratings by order count.
rating_summary["avg_rating_per_order"] = (
    rating_summary["total_ratings"] / rating_summary["order_count"]
)
rating_summary
Out[ ]:
rating restaurant_name 3 4 5 Not given total_ratings order_count avg_rating_per_order
0 'wichcraft 0 0 1 0 1 1 1.000000
1 12 Chairs 0 1 1 2 2 4 0.500000
2 5 Napkin Burger 1 0 1 3 2 5 0.400000
3 67 Burger 0 0 1 0 1 1 1.000000
4 Alidoro 0 0 0 1 0 1 0.000000
... ... ... ... ... ... ... ... ...
173 Zero Otto Nove 0 1 0 1 1 2 0.500000
174 brgr 1 0 0 1 1 2 0.500000
175 da Umberto 0 0 1 2 1 3 0.333333
176 ilili Restaurant 2 7 4 5 13 18 0.722222
177 indikitch 0 1 1 2 2 4 0.500000

178 rows × 8 columns

In [ ]:
# Visualizing the likelihood of an order receving a rating by restaurant volume
sns.barplot(data=rating_summary, x="order_count", y="avg_rating_per_order")
plt.xticks(rotation=90)
plt.title("Average Rating Volume Per Order vs. Order Volume")
Out[ ]:
Text(0.5, 1.0, 'Average Rating Volume Per Order vs. Order Volume')
In [ ]:
# Visualizing the amount of ratings received in total by restaurant volume
sns.barplot(data=rating_summary, x="order_count", y="total_ratings")
plt.xticks(rotation=90)
plt.title("Average Rating Volume vs. Order Volume")
Out[ ]:
Text(0.5, 1.0, 'Average Rating Volume vs. Order Volume')

A key observation:¶

Though the likelihood of a singular order receiving a rating does not necessarily increase with a restaurant's popularity, the overall volume of ratings left does show an exponential increase with order count. A higher volume of ratings spells out higher visibility and thus greater demand.

In [ ]:
# Calculating a dataframe that groups by restaurant name and includes order counts, average food prep time, average cost, and most common day of the week.
# 1. Group by restaurant
grouped = df.groupby("restaurant_name")

# 2. Build summary dataframe
summary = pd.DataFrame({
    "mean_food_prep_time": grouped["food_preparation_time"].mean(),
    "mean_cost": grouped["cost_of_the_order"].mean(),
    "order_count": grouped.size(),   # counts number of rows per restaurant
})

# 3. Add mode of day_of_the_week
summary["mode_day_of_week"] = grouped["day_of_the_week"].agg(
    lambda x: x.mode().iloc[0] if not x.mode().empty else None
)

summary.head()
Out[ ]:
mean_food_prep_time mean_cost order_count mode_day_of_week
restaurant_name
'wichcraft 28.0 8.1000 1 Weekday
12 Chairs 27.0 22.1425 4 Weekday
5 Napkin Burger 30.2 15.9680 5 Weekend
67 Burger 20.0 29.0500 1 Weekday
Alidoro 34.0 12.6100 1 Weekend

Continued Multivariate Analysis¶

In [ ]:
# Visualizing the correlation between restaurant popularity, cost and prep time.
numeric_summary = summary[["mean_food_prep_time", "mean_cost", "order_count"]]
summary_corr = numeric_summary.corr()

plt.figure(figsize=(12,8))
sns.heatmap(summary_corr)
Out[ ]:
<Axes: >
In [ ]:
sns.barplot(data=summary, x="order_count", y="mean_cost", hue="mode_day_of_week")
plt.xticks(rotation=90);

Observations:¶

Restaurants showing a weekday consumer preference are largely concentrated amongst the lowest volume resaurants, never surpassing an order count greater than 18.

In [ ]:
# Visualizing the average cost of an order by restaurant volume
sns.lineplot(data=summary, x="order_count", y="mean_cost")
plt.xticks(rotation=90);
In [ ]:
# Visualizing the average amount of prep time by restaurant volume
sns.lineplot(data=summary, x="order_count", y="mean_food_prep_time")
plt.xticks(rotation=90);

Observations:¶

Visual comparisons of restaurant popularity (order count) with their average order cost and delivery time don't show strong negative or positive correlation.¶

However, restaurants with higher order volumes tend to have more consistent average prices and total wait times.¶

Higher volume ranges: 16-17 dollars, 27-28 minutes¶

Lower volume ranges: 12-23 dollars, 24-31 minutes¶

In [ ]:
# Checking once more for underlaying/overlooked correlations
plt.hexbin(df['food_preparation_time'], df['cost_of_the_order'], gridsize=25)
plt.colorbar(label='Count')
plt.xlabel('food_preparation_time')
plt.ylabel('cost_of_the_order')
Out[ ]:
Text(0, 0.5, 'cost_of_the_order')

Observations:¶

Again the $13 dollar peak is showing as in prior visualizations, with a wider range of prep times between 20 and 35 minutes and higher concentration in the lower range (20-24). There aren't significant differences in costs between weekend and weekday orders.¶

Repeat Customer Analysis¶

As previously shown, there are a smaller amount of individual patron account numbers (Order ID) than there are entries in the data, indicating that there are repeat customers. The following section explores consumer behavior as expressed through loyalty and how it pertains to different volume tiers of business.

In [ ]:
# Rechecking the average timing, cost, and total orders of restaurants by volume.
restaurant_volume_query = """
SELECT
    restaurant_name,
    COUNT(*) AS total_orders,
    ROUND(AVG(cost_of_the_order), 2) AS avg_order_value,
    ROUND(AVG(food_preparation_time + delivery_time), 2) AS avg_total_fulfillment_time
FROM orders
GROUP BY restaurant_name
ORDER BY total_orders DESC;
"""

pd.read_sql(restaurant_volume_query, conn)
Out[ ]:
restaurant_name total_orders avg_order_value avg_total_fulfillment_time
0 Shake Shack 219 16.34 52.61
1 The Meatball Shop 132 16.25 51.42
2 Blue Ribbon Sushi 119 16.00 51.87
3 Blue Ribbon Fried Chicken 96 17.32 51.34
4 Parm 68 16.36 52.81
... ... ... ... ...
173 Balade 1 9.22 49.00
174 Anjappar Chettinad 1 16.44 47.00
175 Alidoro 1 12.61 56.00
176 67 Burger 1 29.05 48.00
177 'wichcraft 1 8.10 56.00

178 rows × 4 columns

In [ ]:
# Labelling restaruants with size-band tiers
restaurant_size_band_query = """
WITH restaurant_orders AS (
    SELECT
        restaurant_name,
        COUNT(*) AS total_orders
    FROM orders
    GROUP BY restaurant_name
)
SELECT
    restaurant_name,
    total_orders,
    CASE
        WHEN total_orders < 10 THEN 'Low-volume'
        WHEN total_orders BETWEEN 10 AND 29 THEN 'Mid-size'
        ELSE 'High-volume'
    END AS restaurant_size_band
FROM restaurant_orders
ORDER BY total_orders DESC;
"""

pd.read_sql(restaurant_size_band_query, conn)
Out[ ]:
restaurant_name total_orders restaurant_size_band
0 Shake Shack 219 High-volume
1 The Meatball Shop 132 High-volume
2 Blue Ribbon Sushi 119 High-volume
3 Blue Ribbon Fried Chicken 96 High-volume
4 Parm 68 High-volume
... ... ... ...
173 Taro Sushi 1 Low-volume
174 The MasalaWala 1 Low-volume
175 Wa Jeal 1 Low-volume
176 Wo Hop Restaurant 1 Low-volume
177 Woorijip 1 Low-volume

178 rows × 3 columns

In [ ]:
# Checking top repeat patrons amidst dataset
customer_restaurant_repeat_query = """
SELECT
    restaurant_name,
    customer_id,
    COUNT(*) AS visits_to_same_restaurant
FROM orders
GROUP BY restaurant_name, customer_id
HAVING COUNT(*) >= 1
ORDER BY visits_to_same_restaurant DESC, restaurant_name;
"""

pd.read_sql(customer_restaurant_repeat_query, conn)
Out[ ]:
restaurant_name customer_id visits_to_same_restaurant
0 Bareburger 47440 6
1 Blue Ribbon Sushi Bar & Grill 97991 6
2 Shake Shack 97806 5
3 The Meatball Shop 84087 5
4 Benihana 97079 4
... ... ... ...
1430 ilili Restaurant 201471 1
1431 ilili Restaurant 301380 1
1432 ilili Restaurant 362961 1
1433 indikitch 7567 1
1434 indikitch 373353 1

1435 rows × 3 columns

In [ ]:
# Checking customer retention rate of restaurants, geenrating repeat customer rate
restaurant_retention_query = """
WITH customer_visits AS (
    SELECT
        restaurant_name,
        customer_id,
        COUNT(*) AS visits
    FROM orders
    GROUP BY restaurant_name, customer_id
),
restaurant_retention AS (
    SELECT
        restaurant_name,
        COUNT(*) AS unique_customers,
        SUM(CASE WHEN visits = 1 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS one_time_customers,
        SUM(CASE WHEN visits >= 2 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS repeat_customers
    FROM customer_visits
    GROUP BY restaurant_name
)
SELECT
    restaurant_name,
    unique_customers,
    one_time_customers,
    repeat_customers,
    ROUND(100.0 * repeat_customers / unique_customers, 2) AS repeat_customer_rate
FROM restaurant_retention
WHERE unique_customers >= 5
ORDER BY repeat_customer_rate DESC, unique_customers DESC;
"""

pd.read_sql(restaurant_retention_query, conn)
Out[ ]:
restaurant_name unique_customers one_time_customers repeat_customers repeat_customer_rate
0 Blue Ribbon Sushi Bar & Grill 25 13 12 48.00
1 The Smile 11 6 5 45.45
2 Vanessa's Dumplings 9 5 4 44.44
3 ilili Restaurant 12 7 5 41.67
4 Blue Ribbon Sushi 75 44 31 41.33
5 Sushi of Gari Tribeca 15 9 6 40.00
6 Osteria Morini 10 6 4 40.00
7 P.J. Clarke's 10 6 4 40.00
8 Sarabeth's Restaurant 5 3 2 40.00
9 Vanessa's Dumpling House 5 3 2 40.00
10 Melt Shop 8 5 3 37.50
11 Sushi Samba 8 5 3 37.50
12 Sushi of Gari 11 7 4 36.36
13 Westville Hudson 11 7 4 36.36
14 Momoya 21 14 7 33.33
15 Otto Enoteca Pizzeria 9 6 3 33.33
16 Boqueria 6 4 2 33.33
17 Cafeteria 6 4 2 33.33

19 Yama Japanese Restaurant 13 9 4 30.77
20 RedFarm Broadway 43 30 13 30.23
21 Hill Country Fried Chicken 10 7 3 30.00
22 Blue Ribbon Fried Chicken 68 48 20 29.41
23 Balthazar Boulangerie 7 5 2 28.57
24 Bareburger 18 13 5 27.78
25 TAO 37 27 10 27.03
26 Nobu Next Door 30 22 8 26.67
27 Jack's Wife Freda 19 14 5 26.32
28 Sushi of Gari 46 28 21 7 25.00
29 J. G. Melon 12 9 3 25.00
30 Tamarind TriBeCa 21 16 5 23.81
31 The Meatball Shop 102 78 24 23.53
32 Shake Shack 170 133 37 21.76
33 Parm 56 44 12 21.43
34 Chipotle Mexican Grill $1.99 Delivery 19 15 4 21.05
35 Cafe Mogador 15 12 3 20.00
36 Empanada Mama (closed) 10 8 2 20.00
37 CafÌ© China 5 4 1 20.00
38 Hatsuhana 5 4 1 20.00
39 L'Express 5 4 1 20.00
40 Han Dynasty 36 29 7 19.44
41 RedFarm Hudson 44 36 8 18.18
42 Blue Ribbon Sushi Izakaya 22 18 4 18.18
43 Mamoun's Falafel 6 5 1 16.67
44 Pepe Rosso To Go 6 5 1 16.67
45 Sarabeth's East 6 5 1 16.67
46 Five Guys Burgers and Fries 25 21 4 16.00
47 Rubirosa 32 27 5 15.63
48 Cafe Habana 13 11 2 15.38
49 The Kati Roll Company 14 12 2 14.29
50 Dos Caminos 7 6 1 14.29
51 Burger Joint 12 12 0 0.00
52 S'MAC 12 12 0 0.00
53 Barbounia 6 6 0 0.00
54 Saravanaa Bhavan 5 5 0 0.00
55 Serafina Fabulous Pizza 5 5 0 0.00
56 Yama 49 5 5 0 0.00
In [ ]:
# Calculating retention averages per size band
retention_by_size_band_query = """
WITH restaurant_orders AS (
    SELECT
        restaurant_name,
        COUNT(*) AS total_orders
    FROM orders
    GROUP BY restaurant_name
),
restaurant_bands AS (
    SELECT
        restaurant_name,
        total_orders,
        CASE
            WHEN total_orders < 10 THEN 'Low-volume'
            WHEN total_orders BETWEEN 10 AND 29 THEN 'Mid-size'
            ELSE 'High-volume'
        END AS restaurant_size_band
    FROM restaurant_orders
),
customer_visits AS (
    SELECT
        restaurant_name,
        customer_id,
        COUNT(*) AS visits
    FROM orders
    GROUP BY restaurant_name, customer_id
),
restaurant_retention AS (
    SELECT
        restaurant_name,
        COUNT(*) AS unique_customers,
        SUM(CASE WHEN visits = 1 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS one_time_customers,
        SUM(CASE WHEN visits >= 2 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS repeat_customers
    FROM customer_visits
    GROUP BY restaurant_name
)
SELECT
    rb.restaurant_size_band,
    COUNT(*) AS num_restaurants,
    ROUND(AVG(rr.unique_customers), 2) AS avg_unique_customers,
    ROUND(AVG(rr.repeat_customers), 2) AS avg_repeat_customers,
    ROUND(AVG(100.0 * rr.repeat_customers / rr.unique_customers), 2) AS avg_repeat_customer_rate
FROM restaurant_bands rb
JOIN restaurant_retention rr
    ON rb.restaurant_name = rr.restaurant_name
WHERE rr.unique_customers >= 5
GROUP BY rb.restaurant_size_band
ORDER BY
    CASE rb.restaurant_size_band
        WHEN 'Low-volume' THEN 1
        WHEN 'Mid-size' THEN 2
        ELSE 3
    END;
"""

pd.read_sql(retention_by_size_band_query, conn)
Out[ ]:
restaurant_size_band num_restaurants avg_unique_customers avg_repeat_customers avg_repeat_customer_rate
0 Low-volume 16 5.56 1.06 19.02
1 Mid-size 27 13.19 3.44 27.77
2 High-volume 14 54.79 14.36 27.21
In [ ]:
# Gathering counts on repeat customers by frequency
deep_repeaters_by_restaurant_query = """
WITH customer_visits AS (
    SELECT
        restaurant_name,
        customer_id,
        COUNT(*) AS visits
    FROM orders
    GROUP BY restaurant_name, customer_id
)
SELECT
    restaurant_name,
    COUNT(*) AS customer_pairs,
    SUM(CASE WHEN visits = 1 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS one_visit_customers,
    SUM(CASE WHEN visits = 2 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS two_visit_customers,
    SUM(CASE WHEN visits = 3 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS three_visit_customers,
    SUM(CASE WHEN visits >= 4 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS four_plus_visit_customers
FROM customer_visits
GROUP BY restaurant_name
HAVING COUNT(*) >= 5
ORDER BY three_visit_customers DESC, four_plus_visit_customers DESC;
"""

pd.read_sql(deep_repeaters_by_restaurant_query, conn)
Out[ ]:
restaurant_name customer_pairs one_visit_customers two_visit_customers three_visit_customers four_plus_visit_customers
0 Shake Shack 170 133 28 7 2
1 Blue Ribbon Fried Chicken 68 48 14 4 2
2 Blue Ribbon Sushi Bar & Grill 25 13 8 3 1
3 The Meatball Shop 102 78 20 3 1
4 RedFarm Hudson 44 36 5 3 0
5 Nobu Next Door 30 22 5 2 1
6 Sushi of Gari 46 28 21 5 2 0

14 Balthazar Boulangerie 7 5 1 1 0
15 Boqueria 6 4 1 1 0

17 Cafeteria 6 4 1 1 0

19 Hill Country Fried Chicken 10 7 2 1 0
20 Jack's Wife Freda 19 14 4 1 0
21 P.J. Clarke's 10 6 3 1 0
22 Sushi of Gari 11 7 3 1 0
23 Tamarind TriBeCa 21 16 4 1 0
24 Xi'an Famous Foods 6 4 1 1 0
25 Yama Japanese Restaurant 13 9 3 1 0
26 ilili Restaurant 12 7 4 1 0
27 Bareburger 18 13 4 0 1
28 Momoya 21 14 6 0 1
29 The Smile 11 6 4 0 1
30 Barbounia 6 6 0 0 0
31 Burger Joint 12 12 0 0 0
32 Cafe Mogador 15 12 3 0 0
33 CafÌ© China 5 4 1 0 0
34 Chipotle Mexican Grill $1.99 Delivery 19 15 4 0 0
35 Dos Caminos 7 6 1 0 0

In [ ]:
# Computing ratio of repeat customers to one time customers amongst data
platform_repeat_mix_query = """
WITH customer_visits AS (
    SELECT
        restaurant_name,
        customer_id,
        COUNT(*) AS visits
    FROM orders
    GROUP BY restaurant_name, customer_id
)
SELECT
    SUM(CASE WHEN visits = 1 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS one_time_customer_restaurant_pairs,
    SUM(CASE WHEN visits >= 2 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS repeat_customer_restaurant_pairs,
    ROUND(
        100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN visits >= 2 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) / COUNT(*),
        2
    ) AS pct_repeat_customer_restaurant_pairs
FROM customer_visits;
"""

pd.read_sql(platform_repeat_mix_query, conn)
Out[ ]:
one_time_customer_restaurant_pairs repeat_customer_restaurant_pairs pct_repeat_customer_restaurant_pairs
0 1076 359 25.02

Key Takeaways:¶

Order volume is concentrated among high-volume restaurants, which has also showed more consistent pricing and fulfillment times. Segmenting by size reveals clear structural differences across the platform.

Repeat behavior is not limited to large restaurants. Mid-size establishments show comparable repeat customer rates, indicating a stronger balance between acquisition and retention, while low-volume restaurants see minimal repeat activity.

At the platform level, repeat engagement is present but limited, with roughly a quarter of customer–restaurant interactions being repeat visits. Most of this activity comes from low-frequency returns rather than strong customer loyalty.

While SQL is used to construct these aggregated datasets efficiently and transparently at the database level, the results are subsequently brought into Python to leverage its more flexible visualization capabilities for clearer comparison and presentation.

In [ ]:
# Creating a table that groups dataset by customer and restaurant
fun = (
    df.groupby(['restaurant_name', 'cuisine_type'])['customer_id']
      .value_counts()                     # how many orders per customer at that restaurant
      .rename('count')                    # give the Series a name
      .reset_index()                      # convert MultiIndex → columns
)
fun
Out[ ]:
restaurant_name cuisine_type customer_id count
0 'wichcraft American 321492 1
1 12 Chairs Middle Eastern 115610 1
2 12 Chairs Middle Eastern 225912 1
3 12 Chairs Middle Eastern 276192 1
4 12 Chairs Middle Eastern 375098 1
... ... ... ... ...
1431 ilili Restaurant Middle Eastern 301380 1
1432 ilili Restaurant Middle Eastern 362961 1
1433 indikitch Indian 353676 2
1434 indikitch Indian 7567 1
1435 indikitch Indian 373353 1

1436 rows × 4 columns

In [ ]:
# Regenerating columns that contain counts of each repeat customer by frequency
fun_pivot = (
    fun
    .groupby(['restaurant_name', 'cuisine_type'])['count']
    .value_counts()            # how many customers with each visit-count
    .unstack(fill_value=0)     # columns 1,2,3,4,5,6
)

# Ensuring columns are 1–6 in order, filling missing with 0
fun_pivot = fun_pivot.reindex(columns=[1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6], fill_value=0)

# Changing column names to be more legible
fun_pivot.columns = [f'count_{c}' for c in fun_pivot.columns]

# Bring index back as columns if you like
fun_pivot = fun_pivot.reset_index()

fun_pivot.head()
Out[ ]:
restaurant_name cuisine_type count_1 count_2 count_3 count_4 count_5 count_6
0 'wichcraft American 1 0 0 0 0 0
1 12 Chairs Middle Eastern 4 0 0 0 0 0
2 5 Napkin Burger American 3 1 0 0 0 0
3 67 Burger American 1 0 0 0 0 0
4 Alidoro American 1 0 0 0 0 0

Note:¶

The above table shows the amount of repeat customers per restaurant, organized by frequency level. For example, a restaurant with an entry of 3 in count_4 means it had 3 customers that placed 4 orders each.¶

In [ ]:
# Adding a column containing total customer counts.
fun_pivot["total_customers"] = (
    fun_pivot[["count_1", "count_2", "count_3", "count_4", "count_5", "count_6"]].sum(axis=1)
)
fun_pivot.head()
Out[ ]:
restaurant_name cuisine_type count_1 count_2 count_3 count_4 count_5 count_6 total_customers
0 'wichcraft American 1 0 0 0 0 0 1
1 12 Chairs Middle Eastern 4 0 0 0 0 0 4
2 5 Napkin Burger American 3 1 0 0 0 0 4
3 67 Burger American 1 0 0 0 0 0 1
4 Alidoro American 1 0 0 0 0 0 1
In [ ]:
# Rearranging table with total_customers going from highest to lowest value.
fun_pivot = (
    fun_pivot
    .sort_values("total_customers", ascending=False)
    .reset_index(drop=True)
)
fun_pivot
Out[ ]:
restaurant_name cuisine_type count_1 count_2 count_3 count_4 count_5 count_6 total_customers

... ... ... ... ... ... ... ... ... ...

In [ ]:
# Generating percentages of repeat customers out of total customers by tier (ordered twice, ordered three times and so forth)
count_cols = [f'count_{i}' for i in range(1, 7)]

for col in count_cols:
    fun_pivot[col + '_pct'] = fun_pivot[col] / fun_pivot['total_customers']

fun_pivot.head(20)

In [ ]:
# Visualizing frequency of 2x returning customers by order volume
sns.lineplot(data=fun_pivot, x ='total_customers', y ='count_2_pct')
Out[ ]:
<Axes: xlabel='total_customers', ylabel='count_2_pct'>

Data amongst 2x repeat customers is very noisy at low volumes, peaking with more stability around 40-80 customers, and then declining beyond 100.

In [ ]:
# Visualizing frequency of 3x returning customers by order volume
sns.lineplot(data=fun_pivot, x ='total_customers', y ='count_3_pct')
Out[ ]:
<Axes: xlabel='total_customers', ylabel='count_3_pct'>

Data amongst 3x repeat customers peaks around 20-40 customers, drops sharply as customers exceed 75 and stays low amongst high-volume restaurants.

In [ ]:
# Visualizing frequency of 4x returning customers by order volume
sns.lineplot(data=fun_pivot, x ='total_customers', y ='count_4_pct')
Out[ ]:
<Axes: xlabel='total_customers', ylabel='count_4_pct'>

Similar to 3x repeat customers, 4x repeat customer probability shows noise at low volumes, has a single outlier spike around the 75 customers and approaches zero at high volumes.

In [ ]:
# Visualizing frequency of 5x returning customers by order volume
sns.lineplot(data=fun_pivot, x ='total_customers', y ='count_5_pct')
Out[ ]:
<Axes: xlabel='total_customers', ylabel='count_5_pct'>
In [ ]:
# Visualizing frequency of 6x returning customers by order volume
sns.lineplot(data=fun_pivot, x ='total_customers', y ='count_6_pct')
Out[ ]:
<Axes: xlabel='total_customers', ylabel='count_6_pct'>

Only small and mid-size restaurants generate ultra loyalty. Large volume restaurants almost never experience 5x and 6x repeat customers.

In [ ]:
# Creating table that showcases all (up to 5x) frequencies of customer return rates
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 7))

# Treating percentage columns as hue
repeat_cols = [
    'count_2_pct',
    'count_3_pct',
    'count_4_pct',
    'count_5_pct',
]
# Generating table
for col in repeat_cols:
    sns.lineplot(
        data=fun_pivot.sort_values('total_customers'),
        x='total_customers',
        y=col,
        label=col
    )

plt.title("Repeat Customer Probability vs. Total Customers")
plt.xlabel("Total Customer Count")
plt.ylabel("Probability of Repeat Behavior")
plt.legend(title="Repeat Count Level")
plt.grid(alpha=0.3)
plt.show()

Observations:¶

High volume restaurants/chains have many visitors who only visit once, as is the basis of their model, thus diluting their loyalty percentages. These business would benefit from investing in customer retention strategies to minimize churn.¶

True loyalty shows up most amongst mid-size establishments which are often niche, specialty and neighborhood hubs. These businesses would benefit further from the implementation of loyalty programs to drill further into their customer basis.¶

Small restaurants show too little data and unstable behavior. They should focus on promotional campaigns to increase exposure in the form of visible ratings and social media advertising.¶

Question 13: The company wants to provide a promotional offer in the advertisement of the restaurants. The condition to get the offer is that the restaurants must have a rating count of more than 50 and the average rating should be greater than 4. Find the restaurants fulfilling the criteria to get the promotional offer. [3 marks]¶

Conclusion and Recommendations¶

Conclusions:¶

  • A significantly higher volume of orders are placed on weekends and delivery drivers move faster to accomodate the higher volumes.

  • Cost of orders, total time to receive orders and ratings tend to show less correlation with one another. Though certain cuisines show light variation and there are slight patterns within their average costs and prep times, it isn't the cuisine itself that has the most impact on popularity per se.

  • The top restaurants that absorb the majority of the market tend to have slightly tighter windows of cost and waiting time, indicating a more streamlined system in place that has greater potential to consistently match customer expectations.

  • Though customer loyalty was shown not to have nearly as high an impact on driving up order volume as the sheer numbers of customer bases, it does tend to show a trend of driving up sales the most for establishments in the mid-ranking level of order volume.

  • One large key factor shown was that there seems to be an evident cycle of higher volumes of ratings being produced by higher volume establishments. Though obvious that this would be the case, it is important to note that we showed there was a drastically higher proportion of ratings given per restaurant in the high-demand cuisines vs their lesser counterparts.

Recommendations:¶

Given that Food Hub makes a percentage on the dollar for all orders delivered, it is in the company's best interest that all restaurants across the board do more business. Please review the following reccomendations:

1. Driving up ratings¶

More ratings spells out more visibility and thus more order volume. Consumers are largely driven by peer reviews and look for a measure of security in their investments of all kinds (even where they order food).

All restaurants and especially those in need of volume should implement better strategies for acquiring ratings, for example:

  • Implementing a points system that rewards leaving non-partisan ratings and builds a credit applied toward future orders.
  • Rewarding consumer interaction for hitting milestones of leaving ratings and reviews.
  • Working on improving their interface to increase the likelihood of each consumer leaving a rating.

2. Bringing more business to weekdays¶

The vast majority of orders are placed on weekends, and its worth noting that weekdays outnumber weekends by 3 days -- this is a lot of wasted potential. They could improve this by:

  • Encouraging business within their database to offer promotional deals on weekdays; happy hour, two-for-one Tuesdays etc. to better drive up sales on these days.
  • Offering reduced surcharges on these days, to further encourage consumers to order in .
  • Rewarding speedy drivers: there tends to be a lag in delivery speeds on weekdays. People are busier on weeknights; if they know they'll get something faster, they're likelier to buy.

3. Campaigning for low-volume establishments¶

Smaller restaurants need to focus on bringing in higher volume more than anything. In addition to bringing strong emphasis to better ratings acquisitions, Foodhub should consider:

  • Offering their platform as a sounding board for advertising and in turn growing business' social media followings through association

  • Offering lower surcharges to restaurants that meet social media campaign and ratings quotas.

Certain restaurants show great models for customer satisfaction, like those of Korean cuisine with their consistently short wait times and affordable prices. Places like these with the right campaign strategy have tons of potential.

4. Tapping into the customer basis potential¶

Mid-size restaurants show the highest retention rates for repeat customers that come back twice, thrice and more. Regulars are the bloodline of niche, mid-size neighborhood establishments. This can be maximized by:

  • Encouraging businesses to implement loyalty programs that are accessed and managed through Foodhub's platform.

On the flip-side, large businesses with models based on volume thrive by acquiring large scales of single visit customers through their broad reach. They could focus on even further maximizing business by reducing customer churn. Foodhub's strategies for this might include:

  • Also encouraging loyalty programs through their platform

  • Increasing 'stickiness' by streamlining user selection ('Order it again') and bundling options ('Add Shakeshack on for dessert with only $1.00 additional delivery fee!')

  • Designing volume-based incentives, such as combo meals and discounts (buy 2 get 1 free).

5. Encouraging consistency¶

The biggest players in the dataset show the most consistency in their pricing and timing. This could be improved for the entire basis by measures such as:

  • Encouraging businesses to limit their menu availability (reserving time-consuming dishes for dine-in only).

  • Improving onboarding for new businesses ('These time windows, price margins, dish types and so forth make for an ideal Foodhub profile..').

  • Rewarding and promoting businesses that hit consistency marks best.
